claim: A precision extensometer and strain gauges were used to measure the elastic modulus of SPCE sheet metal from loading/unloading stress-strain curves during tensile tests up to 25% plastic strain.
evidence_text: A precision extensometer was developed by the authors, and a tensile test using the extensometer and the strain gages was carried out for measuring the elastic modulus of SPCE sheet metal before and after large deformation.

claim: In V-bending, finite element simulations using the plastic-strain-dependent elastic modulus (varied E) predict spring-back values closer to experimental measurements than simulations using a constant initial elastic modulus.
evidence_text: The results show that the spring-back with the varied elastic modulus could be predicted more accurately than that with the constant initial elastic modulus.

claim: Specimens were heat-treated at 373 K for 90 hours to recover the Cottrell atmosphere via carbon diffusion while keeping dislocations stationary, with the diffusion time calculated using X=(2Dt)^(1/2).
evidence_text: The condition of heat treatment were 373 K for 90 h for recovering only the Cottrell atmosphere due to the diffusion of C and whilst keeping the dislocation stationary. The conditions were calculated based on the average diffusion distance x of solute C in GnFe matrix in time t by means of following equation: X = (2 D t) ^ {1 / 2}.

claim: Only the annealed SPCC specimen maintained a nearly constant elastic modulus until yielding, while as-received and pre-strained specimens showed a decreasing modulus from the beginning of loading.
evidence_text: The elastic modulus remains almost the same until yielding for the annealed workpiece only. The gradient of the stress–strain curve decrease from the beginning up to the macroscopic yield point, so that the modulus varied significantly for the workpiece with 15% pre-strain and even for the as-received workpiece.

claim: Using a spherical indenter tip with nominal radius of 50 μm, the nano-indentation load–displacement curve shows an indentation depth as small as several nanometers; at a load of approximately 350 μN the penetration depth is about 10 nm.
evidence_text: Fig. 8 shows one of the load–displacement curves with a nominal radius of R = 50 μm. The indentation depth was as small as several nanometers as shown in Fig. 8.

claim: Nano-indentation measurements were performed over a rectangular area with an interval distance of 1 μm in X direction, total 20 points along X, and 3 lines in Y direction (Y=0,1,2), resulting in 60 measurement points.
evidence_text: The measurement was performed with an interval distance of 1 μm, and the total measurement points are 20 and 3 in X and Y directions, respectively.
